title: Ceasefire Collapses, Diesel Markets Spike, and Ukraine Burns Russia's Shadow Fleet
subhead: Trump declared the Iran ceasefire 'over' after fresh strikes on 80+ Iranian targets, Russia banned diesel exports as Ukrainian drones torched shadow fleet tankers, and European diesel margins hit a record $60/bbl — a day where three overlapping fuel crises converged.

sections.heading: Iran Ceasefire Declared 'Over' as US Strikes 80+ Targets and Gulf States Get Hit
sections.body:
President Trump declared the eight-week US-Iran ceasefire 'over' on Wednesday, hours after Iran struck three commercial vessels near the Strait of Hormuz — a Qatari LNG carrier (Al Rekayyat), a Saudi crude tanker, and a Liberian-flagged oil tanker. The US responded with strikes on over 80 Iranian military targets, including air defence systems, command and control networks, coastal radar sites, anti-ship missile capabilities, and more than 60 IRGC small boats, according to US CENTCOM as reported by Sky News.

Iranian forces retaliated by striking US military bases in Bahrain and Kuwait. Sirens sounded at least twice in Bahrain, home to the US Navy's 5th Fleet. Kuwait's military said it was actively intercepting incoming drones and missiles. Iran's Revolutionary Guard claimed responsibility for the attacks on both Gulf states. There was no immediate word on damage in the three targeted countries (Bahrain, Kuwait, and Qatar), according to the Associated Press via Newsday.

Iranian state media reported explosions in Bushehr (home to Iran's nuclear power plant), the southern port cities of Chabahar, Konarak, Bandar Abbas, and Sirik, and in Iranshahr where a firefighter was killed at an airport. For the first time since April, US strikes also appeared to target Iranian bridges — state media reported a strike on a railway bridge in Golestan province, and the IRGC said two bridges were attacked on the route to Mashhad, where funeral preparations for the late Ayatollah Khamenei were underway.

Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf, a key peace-negotiation figure, struck a defiant tone: 'America still has not learned that bullying and breaking promises are no longer cost-free. If you strike, you will get hit.'

Brent crude settled at $79.28/bbl, with WTI near $74.55/bbl — both at multi-week highs. Oil prices had surged nearly 5% during the session, according to CNBC Africa and Seeking Alpha. The IEA's 400 million barrel strategic reserve release had provided temporary relief earlier in the year, but that buffer is being exhausted, according to CryptoBriefing's analysis of IEA data.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire that briefly calmed Hormuz has collapsed into a direct military exchange engulfing Gulf Arab states for the first time. With Iran asserting its right to manage Hormuz traffic under the now-defunct deal, and the US striking inland infrastructure including bridges near funeral processions, the escalation cycle is deepening beyond the shipping lane into a broader regional war.

sections.heading: Damaged Qatari LNG Tanker Highlights Hormuz's Existential Risk to Gas Markets
sections.body:
The Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat remained stranded off Oman on Wednesday after a projectile strike sparked a fire in its engine room, Reuters reported. The crew was safely evacuated. Industry sources told Reuters the vessel was at risk of exploding, though there was no indication the LNG cargo tanks themselves had been breached. One industry source said that if the vessel is not hit again, it will likely remain in its present condition and not explode — the danger would escalate only if fire reaches cargo systems or causes a major LNG leak.

The incident marks the second LNG ship this year to be affected by a war-related strike. In March, the Russian LNG tanker Arctic Metagaz caught fire in the Mediterranean after being hit by Ukrainian naval drones, forcing its crew to abandon ship, according to Russia's transport ministry as cited by Pakistan Today.

Iranian state media said Al Rekayyat was struck 'after ignoring repeated warnings' — a claim that, if true, signals Iran is actively enforcing a toll-and-rerouting regime on commercial gas traffic, not merely threatening it. MST Marquee's head of research Saul Kavonic told Reuters: 'The current conflagration is a reminder to the market of how fragile passage through the Strait still is.'
sections.whyItMatters: An LNG carrier damaged by military action in the world's most critical energy chokepoint is no longer theoretical — it has happened. Qatar supplies roughly 25% of global LNG. If Al Rekayyat had suffered a cargo-tank breach, the consequences for the LNG market, European winter gas supplies, and global insurance would have been severe and immediate. The incident raises the question of when — not whether — a major LNG shipment is lost in this conflict.

sections.heading: Russia Bans Diesel Exports as Ukrainian Drones Torch Shadow Fleet Tankers
sections.body:
Russia imposed a full ban on diesel exports on Wednesday, effective until July 31, according to Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak, who announced the measure at a government meeting chaired by President Putin. Russia also plans to begin importing fuel this month — a striking reversal for one of the world's largest energy exporters. Novak acknowledged that 'the fuel situation remains complex' and that conditions at filling stations 'are causing concern among the public,' as reported by Ukrainska Pravda and Yahoo News.

The ban formalises what traders say was already reality. According to Kpler data cited by Firstpost, Russian seaborne diesel and gasoil exports fell 39% in June from the previous month to around 1.8 million metric tonnes — a 46% drop from June 2025. Between July 1-8, Russian diesel and gasoil exports averaged just 214,000 bpd, compared with 793,000 bpd in July 2025 and 842,000 bpd in July 2021.

The export ban sent global diesel markets into a spike. Benchmark European diesel refining margins surged to a record $60.17 per barrel, according to Devdiscourse. US diesel futures posted their biggest daily gain in four years, according to Reuters via MSN. Russia had already banned petrol exports in April.

Meanwhile, Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces reported hitting more than nine Russian shadow fleet tankers in the Sea of Azov overnight Tuesday-Wednesday, the third consecutive night of naval drone strikes. Ukraine claims 21 civilian vessels have been damaged since Monday — including 19 shadow fleet tankers, one dry cargo ship, and one automobile ferry. According to Kyiv Post, the identified vessels include Venera-3, Sanar-1, Sanar-17, Climene, Teti, Alexey Savrasov, and Penelope — all Russian-flagged with an average deadweight of 7,000 tons, linked to sanctioned crude transport. Separately, Ukrainian drones struck the Saratov oil refinery (killing one person) and the Nizhnekamskneftekhim/TAIF-NK complex in Tatarstan, and set fire to the Borisoglebsk military airfield in the Voronezh region, according to independent Russian outlet Astra and Meduza. Russia's Defense Ministry claimed it shot down 415 Ukrainian drones overnight.

Several Russian regions have introduced odd-even rationing at petrol stations based on vehicle registration numbers, according to Ukrainska Pravda. Independent Russian media confirmed fuel shortages persist in Crimea after two laden tankers failed to reach the peninsula.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia — the world's second-largest oil exporter — is now importing fuel and rationing domestic supply. Ukraine's drone campaign has moved from degrading refinery capacity to attacking the maritime logistics chain that moves Russian oil, hitting an estimated 19 shadow fleet tankers in three nights. The global diesel market, already tight from Hormuz disruptions, just lost its single largest diesel supplier from the export market. Refiners with access to non-Russian, non-Hormuz crude are enjoying record margins; everyone else faces a supply crunch.

sections.heading: India-Australia Uranium Deal Advances as Global Nuclear Ambitions Intensify
sections.body:
Prime Minister Narendra Modi addressed the Economic Roadmap Business Reception in Melbourne on Wednesday alongside Australian PM Anthony Albanese, calling India and Australia 'natural and trusted partners' at a time of global energy insecurity. The centrepiece of the visit: a landmark uranium supply agreement that would relax 12-year-old regulatory barriers on Australian uranium exports to India, according to the Australian Financial Review.

Modi said India targets 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030 and 100 GW of nuclear power by 2047, having opened its nuclear sector to private participation. Australia's vast uranium reserves — among the world's largest — would contribute significantly to India's long-term energy security and decarbonisation, he argued. The AFR reported that uranium miners are 'set to cash in' on the deal, which comes after more than a decade since the original civil nuclear cooperation pact was signed between the two countries.

The deal is being positioned against the backdrop of Middle Eastern supply disruption: Modi explicitly cited energy insecurity as a driver for the partnership, and the timing — during the collapse of the Iran ceasefire — underscores how Gulf volatility is accelerating nuclear deals that might otherwise have taken years to finalise. Bilateral trade has crossed $25 billion annually, supported by the India-Australia Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement (ECTA) signed in 2022, according to MSN India.
sections.whyItMatters: The India-Australia uranium deal is the latest sign that the Iran-Hormuz crisis is accelerating civil nuclear commitments globally. Countries that can lock in uranium supply agreements now are hedging against fossil fuel chokepoint risk. Australia's willingness to relax export barriers — over a decade in the making — reflects how the energy security calculus has shifted since the Strait of Hormuz became a war zone.

sections.heading: IMF Cuts 2026 Global Growth to 3%, Warns Gulf Conflict Could Extend Commodity Volatility
sections.body:
The IMF cut its 2026 global growth forecast to 3.0%, down from 3.1% in April, warning that the AI boom has not fully offset the fallout from the Middle East war. The revision was made before the latest US-Iran escalation, the fund noted in its World Economic Outlook update released Wednesday.

Global inflation is now projected at 4.7% this year, higher than previously forecast. The IMF expects growth to rebound to 3.4% in 2027, describing the pattern as a 'V-shaped recovery' — but only if the Strait of Hormuz normalises by then, according to IMF research division chief Deniz Igan, speaking to AFP.

The fund highlighted glaring regional disparities. The US economy is still set to grow 2.3%, while the Middle East and Central Asia forecast was cut by 1.2 percentage points to 0.7%. The euro area is projected at 0.9% growth, with France downgraded 0.3 points to 0.6%. China's projection was nudged up to 4.6%. Retail gasoline costs jumped 30% in emerging Asia after the war's onset, versus only 15% in Latin America, according to Deccan Chronicle's coverage of the IMF report.

The IMF warned that 'the possibility of a renewed Middle East conflict looms large and could extend commodity price volatility, further threaten supply chains, raise prices, and weigh on financial conditions.' It noted that strategic petroleum reserve releases have provided relief, but that weakness ahead is possible as those buffers are drawn down.
sections.whyItMatters: The IMF's assessment makes clear what market participants already sense: the Gulf conflict is a drag on global growth that hits energy importers disproportionately. Emerging Asia's 30% gasoline spike versus Latin America's 15% shows how the same shock lands unevenly. The fund's admission that its own numbers don't yet account for the latest escalation suggests the next revision could be significantly worse.

sections.heading: Vitol Moves Into Venezuela as Post-Maduro Oil Rush Accelerates
sections.body:
Commodity trading major Vitol is planning to establish a presence in Venezuela, Reuters reported on Tuesday, citing unnamed sources. Vitol's head of Latin America, Henry Medina, told Reuters: 'For many years Vitol has had a strong relationship with PDVSA. We look forward to building on this and developing additional partnerships in Venezuela, as well as a meaningful presence in the country.'

Vitol and Trafigura had already struck a White House-backed deal to sell 50 million barrels of Venezuelan crude, valued at around $2 billion, after the US ousted Venezuelan president Nicolas Maduro in January and brought him to the US to face drug trafficking charges. That total has since been increased to 100 million barrels, according to Oilprice.com. The biggest target buyers have been Chinese and Indian refiners, with some oil offered at a substantial discount to Brent.

Venezuela exported an estimated 1.25 million bpd in May — the highest since 2019 — with the US, India, and Europe as major buyers. Production and exports are expected to rise further as international firms return, though output remains a third of the peak 3 million bpd from over a decade ago.

The timing is deliberate: with Middle Eastern supply disrupted by the Iran-Hormuz crisis, Venezuela's oil is suddenly more attractive to Washington (as a non-Iranian, non-Russian alternative) and to traders who can profit from the discount. The White House deal explicitly positions Venezuelan crude as a partial substitute for lost Hormuz volumes.
sections.whyItMatters: Venezuela's post-Maduro oil reintegration is accelerating because of the Gulf crisis, not despite it. The US is effectively subsidising a former pariah oil state to offset the loss of Iranian and Hormuz-disrupted supply. Whether Venezuela can sustain 1.25 million bpd — let alone climb back toward 3 million — depends on whether the political settlement holds and whether international investment arrives at scale.

sections.heading: France's Heatwave Threatens Nuclear Output as Europe Faces Dual Energy Squeeze
sections.body:
EDF warned that as many as five French nuclear reactors could face output cuts as the country's latest heatwave raises river temperatures beyond cooling-water limits, Bloomberg reported on Tuesday. France's third major heatwave this summer comes at a moment when European power systems are already under strain from reduced LNG supply through Hormuz and low gas storage levels.

EU gas storage stood at 28-47% of capacity — the lowest in 15 years for this time of year, according to Yahoo News via the Economist. France, which derives roughly 70% of its electricity from nuclear power, is particularly exposed when river temperatures prevent reactors from discharging cooling water. The Copernicus Climate Change Service reported that June was the hottest on record for Western Europe, nearly 5.5 degrees above average, according to MSN.

The convergence of heatwave-driven nuclear curtailments and Hormuz-related LNG disruption creates a compound risk: less nuclear output increases gas demand for electricity generation, just as LNG supply to Europe is constricted. If France must curtail nuclear generation during peak cooling demand, the knock-on effect on European power prices and gas storage refill rates will compound an already difficult winter preparation cycle.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe is caught between a physical climate shock (heatwave-driven nuclear curtailment) and a geopolitical supply shock (Hormuz LNG disruption). France's nuclear fleet is the continent's baseload backbone — if five reactors reduce output during peak summer demand, gas-to-power switching will deplete already-low storage further, making winter 2026-27 prep that much harder.

bottomLine: Three fuel crises converged on July 9: the Iran ceasefire collapsed into direct US-Iran-Gulf state military exchanges, Russia banned diesel exports as Ukrainian drones burned its shadow fleet tankers and struck inland refineries, and Europe's heatwave threatens to cut nuclear output at the exact moment LNG supply is squeezed. The global diesel market hit a record $60/bbl margin, Brent topped $79, and the IMF cut world growth to 3% — before the latest escalation. India and Australia moved to lock in a uranium supply deal, Vitol expanded into Venezuela, and a damaged Qatari LNG tanker sat stranded off Oman as a live reminder that Hormuz can take LNG off the market, not just oil. The overlapping crises are no longer theoretical — they are manifesting as rationing in Russia, record refining margins in Europe, and 30% gasoline spikes across emerging Asia.
sources: Associated Press via Newsday, Reuters, CNBC Africa, Seeking Alpha, Oilprice.com, Kyiv Post, Meduza, Ukrainska Pravda, Firstpost, Devdiscourse, Yahoo News, Pakistan Today, CryptoBriefing, Deccan Chronicle, IMF World Economic Outlook Update, Australian Financial Review, MSN India, Bloomberg, Copernicus Climate Change Service, MSN, Fox News, Financial Express, The Hindu, AFR, WSJ, Economist
